titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the James Barry Papers
titlestmt.author: by Sandra Markham

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday November 22, 2023 at 02:25

did.abstract: The James Barry Papers contain correspondence and documents concerning Barry's financial affairs, artistic ventures, and letters from his brothers Redmond and Patrick. There are letters from some of his colleagues including the painters Peter Benazech and William Keable. The majority of the collection, however, concerns attorney (and cousin) Daniel Reardon and Barry's family's handling of his estate after his death, in particular his sister Mary Ann Bulkley's efforts reprint and market his engravings. Also present are probate inventories of Barry's home and studio, correspondence with auctioneer James Christie, and two auction catalogues of the Barry sale annotated with prices realized. The Reardon correspondence contains a letter from Mary Ann Bulkley's daughter Margaret Ann describing her life once she had transitioned to become "James Barry."

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: W. S. Lewis purchased the papers from Bland Gallery, Inc., in June 1950; bequest of Wilmarth Sheldon Lewis (Yale 1918), 1979.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three series: I. Professional and Personal Papers, 1765-1842. II. Estate Papers, 1806-1824. III. Barry Family Papers, 1808-1884.

bioghist.head: James Barry (1741-1806)
bioghist.p: James Barry (1741-1806) was a history painter, printmaker, and author. At 22, he exhibited his picture The Baptism of the King of Cashel by St. Patrick at the Dublin Society for the Encouragement of Arts, Manufactures, and Commerce. Edmund Burke and William Burke became his patrons, arranging for him to study in Europe. When Barry returned to England, he was invited to join The Royal Academy. Barry became his own printmaker, publisher, and distributor by 1776, and was also a writer, especially concerning the state of the arts in England, as demonstrated by his first book, An Inquiry into the Real and Imaginary Obstructions to the Acquisition of the Arts in England (1775). In 1782, he became professor of painting at the Royal Academy. However, Barry's last major publication, A Letter to the Dilettanti Society of 1798, appealed to the Society to promote the arts to compensate for what he considered to be the Royal Academy's lack of leadership, and in which he inveighed angrily against his colleagues. For these and other remarks in his lectures, he was expelled from the Royal Academy on 15 April 1799, and died in poverty in 1806..

bioghist.head: Dr. James Barry (1799-1865)
bioghist.p: James Barry (1799-1865), a transgender army medical officer, was born Margaret Ann Bulkley, the daughter of Mary Anne Barry Bulkley and Jeremiah Bulkley; she was also the niece of the artist James Barry (1741-1806). In 1809 Mary Ann Bulkley settled in Edinburgh with her daughter, from then on known as James Barry, who matriculated as a literary and medical student at the university. Barry lived his adult life as a man, receiving his MD in 1812. Barry arrived in the Cape in 1816, and in 1817 was appointed physician to the household of the governor, Lord Charles Somerset. During the winter of 1819 Barry paid a secret visit to the island of Mauritius because, it has been suggested, he had become pregnant by Somerset and went there to give birth. Barry was promoted to staff surgeon in 1827 and in 1828 was appointed to Mauritius. In 1858 he became inspector-general of hospitals. After contracting severe influenza in Canada, Barry returned to England in 1859, retired on half pay, and died in 1865. His death certificate lists Barry's sex as male.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: James Barry Papers. The Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The James Barry Papers is the physical property of the Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the W. S. Lewis Librarian/Executive Director.

corpname.part: Society for the Encouragement of Arts, Manufactures, and Commerce (Great Britain)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into five subseries: Professional Correspondence and Ephemera, Personal Correspondence and Ephemera, Family Correspondence, Financial Records, and Writings.

did.unittitle: Draft note from Barry about his fourth lecture
Invitation to Barry attend a meeting on March 19
Society for the Encouragement of Arts, Manufactures and Commerce

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes two drafts of letters (one from Charles Taylor) to Barry, one draft letter, and one draft by Barry to the Society written on the verso of a printed 1792 announcement of his engravings from his paintings for the Society. Also present is a draft of a letter from Barry to Samuel More, responding to a SEAMC meeting notice.

did.unittitle: Draft of a letter to David Stewart Erskine, Earl of Buchan, 1742-1829

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Draft letter written on the verso of a printed invitation to a May 1805 meeting of the SEAMC Committee of Polite Arts.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Requesting the return of Barry's "A Letter to the Dilettanti Society of 1798" to the Society.

did.unittitle: Committee of Polite Arts meeting minutes

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Benazech was an engraver and painter in watercolours.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inscribed on verso by Barry: "Taken up at Mr Towgood, 101.8.2. May 27, 1800."

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Burroughs was the author of A Poetical Epistle to James Barry, Esq. (1805).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A calligraphic transcription of an October 5, 1800, letter from Barry to J. C. Fox, written on paper watermarked "Ludlow 1842."

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Keable was an English painter of portraits and conversation pieces.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sent to Barry in Parma, Italy.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Neve was an author who wrote under the name "One of the Magistrates of the Public Office in Gt. Marlborough Street."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Concerning being admitted as a Brother in the Society of Saint Patrick in Dublin.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Draft letter to George Tennant written on the verso of an undated dinner invitation to Barry from Sharon Turner (1768-1847).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fragment of a letter sent to Barry on Suffolk Street that mentions his "Venus and Adonis."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letter from an Irish artist in London, sent to Barry on Suffolk Street, and writing about sending pictures from Dublin for exhibition at the Royal Academy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Incomplete letter to Barry (?) discussing Barry and Lord Bacon.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The writer, possibly a Count de Schepaux, refers to the recent death of George Bussy Villiers, 4th Earl of Jersey, on August 22, 1805. He also mentions the Lambtons, John Wharton, and Bonomi's son Ignatius.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The subseries holds letters from two of Barry's three living siblings: his brothers Patrick and Redmond, and his sister Mary Ann, plus her husband Jeremiah.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Patrick Barry was the brother of James Barry. He also used the name Patrick Riordan, a variant of his mother's maiden name of Reardon. In 1771 he enlisted in the marines but deserted the following year. Afterward, he entered the service of the East India Company. By 1789 he was again in London, and then returned to the East Indies, where he died.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also present is a capsule biography of Patrick, possibly written by Barry, on a scrap of paper.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Redmond was the oldest of the three brothers. He served in the Royal Navy but ran into legal and financial trouble. He sold his claim to Barry's estate to his sister, and died in poverty in 1824.

did.unittitle: Rental contracts for Castle Street house, and invoice for repairs
Invoice and receipt for a copper plate bought from Thomas Large
Invoice for paper bought from John Booth
Receipt for food purchased from Guedon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pages and fragments of writings.

did.unittitle: Essay for the preface to A Description of the Series of Pictures Painted by James Barry, Esq.
A Description of the Series of Pictures Painted by James Barry, Esq. (London: Printed by C. Whittingham, 1803)
"The Two Books of Francis Bacon on the Advancement &c of Learning, London. 1605"

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is organized into two subseries: Documents, and Daniel Reardon Correspondence and Receipts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains material collected and generated by Daniel Reardon, who served as the Barry family's lawyer in London and was as well cousin on the family's maternal side. Filed here are the inventories taken of Barry's personal effects, and correspondence with James Christie, who auctioned the material in two sales in April 1807. Present are two catalogues, each annotated with prices realized.

Notable in Reardon's correspondence is a letter to him from Margaret Ann Bulkley, who had enrolled at University of Edinburgh as a man, James Barry, in order to study medicine. The letter describes Barry's student life and surroundings.

did.unittitle: "Money of the late Mr Barry found in a packet on his person at the time of his Death"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inventory and receipt for money turned over to Mary Ann Bulkley and Redmond Barry, dated August 12, 1806. Annotated on verso: Mary A. Bulkley received Barry's silver snuff box, gold pencil, and small silver coins on July 20, 1807.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One full inventory of possessions and books, with a fair copy of some items.

did.unittitle: Invoices and receipts for estate settlement in the Court of Canterbury
Declaration of Bulkley family financial status, Court of Canterbury
Copy of Deed of Trust between Redmond Barry and Alexander Poulden

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Daniel Reardon (or Riordan), a cousin, was the Barry family's attorney in London.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: James Barry was the name taken by Margaret Anne Bulkley, the daughter of Mary Ann Barry Bulkley, in 1809, in order to matriculate at the University of Edinburgh to study medicine. Barry maintained a masculine appearance and lifestyle through death, and was at times identified as the nephew of Mary Ann Bulkley.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Bulkley was the husband of Mary Ann Barry.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three letters asking the whereabouts of Bulkley's wife and daughter, and one letter mailed to Margaret Ann Bulkeley in care of Reardon.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains letters to and from Bulkley, along with Reardon's financial statements and receipts.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two printed catalogues for paintings, drawings, sketches, prints, casts, copper plates, and books sold in two auctions on April 10 and 11, 1807. Both were annotated with prices realized.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Francis Douce (1757-1834) was an antiquarian and book collector, and was a keeper of the manuscripts at the British Museum from circa 1807 to 1811.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A note possibly in Douce's hand regarding the sorting of Barry's papers.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series III is organized into two subseries: Correspondence, and Documents.

did.unittitle: Anderson, John, to Mary Ann Bulkley
Bulkley, John, to Jeremiah Bulkley and Mary Ann Bulkley

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: John Bulkley was the son of Jeremiah and Mary Ann Bulkley.

did.unittitle: Bulkley, Margaret Ann, to John Bulkley

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Margaret Ann Bulkley (later to become Dr. James Barry) writing to her brother John.

did.unittitle: Fryer, Edward, to Mary Ann Bulkley

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Physician Edward Fryer (1761-1826) became a close friend of James Barry, and his first biographer.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also present is Bulkley's copy of her circa 1807 note to Fryer, and a copy, in Fryer's hand, of Bulkley's undated letter to William Long Wellesley, Earl of Mornington (1788-1857).

did.unittitle: Print dealers and booksellers receipts and letters to Mary Ann Bulkley

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Regarding the distribution of Barry's reprinted engravings, and their lack of sales.

did.unittitle: Subscription announcement for Redmond Barry relief fund

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The fund was organized by a committee of Society of Arts members; the circular was printed by the Lithographic Press and features a portrait of Barry by William Bewick.

did.unittitle: Mary Ann Bulkley's draft of dedication page for Barry engravings
Printed prospectus for the reprint of Barry's engravings
A Description of the Series of Pictures Painted by the Late James Barry Esq. (London: 1833)
"A Mystery Still," in All the Year Round 17, no. 421 (May 18, 1867)
Spines from albums one and two
